title: High Pressure Protection
shortDescription: The system detected high refrigerant pressure and shut down to prevent compressor damage.
fullDescription: Error code E1 on a Carrier air conditioner indicates the high pressure safety switch has tripped. Refrigerant pressure exceeded the safe operating range, which can be caused by restricted airflow, dirty condenser coils, or overcharged refrigerant.
safetyWarning: High voltage is present in AC systems. Turn off power at the disconnect before inspecting. Never attempt to add or release refrigerant without EPA certification.

causes.cause: Restricted airflow — dirty filter or blocked vents

symptoms: Outdoor unit may be very hot
Compressor stops but fan may still run

fixSteps.title: Clean Condenser Coils
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the AC. Use a garden hose to gently spray the outdoor unit coils from inside out to remove dirt, leaves, and debris.

fixSteps.title: Check Air Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Replace or clean the indoor air filter if dirty.

fixSteps.title: Check Condenser Fan
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the outdoor fan spins freely and runs when the AC is on.

fixSteps.title: Clear Area Around Unit
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure at least 2 feet of clearance around the outdoor unit. Trim vegetation and remove debris.

whenToCallPro: If the coils are clean, fan works, and the error persists. Refrigerant levels need a professional with gauges.

faq.q: Can I fix high pressure myself?
faq.a: You can clean coils and check airflow. Refrigerant issues require an EPA-certified technician.

faq.q: Will the AC reset itself?
faq.a: Most Carrier units will auto-reset after the pressure drops. You can also cycle the power.

faq.q: How often should I clean the condenser?
faq.a: At least once a year, ideally in spring before cooling season.

title: Indoor Anti-Freeze Protection
shortDescription: The evaporator coil temperature dropped too low, triggering freeze protection.
fullDescription: E2 indicates the indoor evaporator coil is at or near freezing temperature. The system shuts down the compressor to prevent ice formation on the coil, which can damage the compressor.
safetyWarning: Turn off the system and let any ice melt completely (may take several hours) before restarting. Running a frozen system can damage the compressor.

causes.cause: Dirty air filter severely restricting airflow
Blower motor running too slow or failing

symptoms: Ice visible on refrigerant lines or indoor coil

fixSteps.title: Turn Off and Thaw
fixSteps.instruction: Turn the AC to fan-only mode (or off) and let ice melt completely. This may take 2-4 hours.

fixSteps.title: Replace Air Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Check and replace the indoor filter. A clogged filter is the most common cause.

fixSteps.title: Check Vents
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure all supply and return vents are open and unblocked.

fixSteps.title: Restart and Monitor
fixSteps.instruction: After all ice has melted and filter is replaced, restart the AC and monitor for 30 minutes.

whenToCallPro: If the problem recurs after replacing the filter, it likely indicates low refrigerant from a leak that needs professional repair.

faq.q: Why is my AC freezing up?
faq.a: Usually a dirty filter or low refrigerant. Less commonly, a failing blower motor or dirty coil.

faq.q: How long to thaw?
faq.a: 2-4 hours in fan-only mode. Do not try to chip or melt ice with heat.

faq.q: Can running a frozen AC damage it?
faq.a: Yes, it can damage the compressor. Always let the ice melt completely before restarting.

title: Low Pressure Protection
shortDescription: Low refrigerant pressure detected — possible refrigerant leak.
fullDescription: E3 indicates the low-pressure safety switch has tripped, meaning refrigerant pressure is below the safe operating range. This is commonly caused by a refrigerant leak or restriction in the system.
safetyWarning: Refrigerant handling requires EPA certification. Do not attempt to add refrigerant yourself. Exposure to refrigerant can cause frostbite.
symptoms: AC runs but does not cool well
Ice on the outdoor unit or refrigerant lines

fixSteps.title: Check for Obvious Issues
fixSteps.instruction: Look for ice on the refrigerant lines, hissing sounds, or oil stains near connections which indicate a leak.

fixSteps.title: Check Air Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Although primarily a refrigerant issue, check the filter to rule out airflow problems contributing to low pressure.

fixSteps.title: Check Outdoor Temperature
fixSteps.instruction: Running AC when outdoor temp is below 60F can cause low pressure trips on some units. This is normal.

fixSteps.title: Call for Leak Check
fixSteps.instruction: A professional must check refrigerant levels, locate any leaks, repair them, and recharge the system.

whenToCallPro: For any E3 code. Low pressure almost always indicates a refrigerant issue requiring professional service.

faq.q: Can I add refrigerant myself?
faq.a: No, refrigerant handling requires EPA Section 608 certification. It is also illegal for homeowners to purchase and add refrigerant.

faq.q: How much does a refrigerant leak repair cost?
faq.a: $200-$1,500 depending on the leak location and type of refrigerant.

faq.q: Is low refrigerant normal?
faq.a: No, AC systems are sealed and should not lose refrigerant over time. Low refrigerant always means there is a leak.

title: Compressor Discharge Temperature Too High
shortDescription: The compressor is running too hot, indicating a system problem.
fullDescription: E4 indicates the compressor discharge temperature has exceeded the safe limit. This can damage the compressor if not addressed promptly.
safetyWarning: Turn off the AC to prevent compressor damage. The outdoor unit and refrigerant lines may be extremely hot.
symptoms: Compressor noisy or hot to touch

fixSteps.title: Turn Off AC
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the system to protect the compressor from heat damage.

fixSteps.title: Clean Condenser
fixSteps.instruction: After the unit cools, clean the outdoor coils with a garden hose.

fixSteps.title: Check Fan
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the outdoor fan motor runs and spins the blade freely.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: If cleaning does not resolve it, a tech needs to check refrigerant levels and compressor health.

whenToCallPro: If cleaning the condenser does not fix the issue. Compressor issues require professional diagnosis.

faq.q: Can a hot compressor be fixed?
faq.a: Often yes, by addressing the root cause (dirty coils, low charge, fan failure). But a compressor with internal damage may need replacement.

faq.q: How much is a new compressor?
faq.a: $1,500-$3,000+ installed. Check your warranty — many compressors have 5-10 year warranties.

faq.q: Is this urgent?
faq.a: Yes, running an overheating compressor can cause permanent damage.

title: Overcurrent Protection — Compressor
shortDescription: The compressor is drawing too much electrical current.
fullDescription: E5 indicates the compressor is drawing excessive current, which triggers the overcurrent protection to prevent electrical damage or fire.
safetyWarning: High voltage hazard. Do not work on electrical components without turning off the disconnect switch. Overcurrent can indicate a serious electrical problem.

causes.cause: Failing compressor motor (worn bearings, shorted windings)
Low voltage at the outdoor unit
Dirty condenser coils making compressor work harder

symptoms: Compressor hums but does not start

fixSteps.title: Clean Condenser
fixSteps.instruction: Clean outdoor coils to reduce compressor workload.

fixSteps.title: Check Breaker
fixSteps.instruction: If the breaker trips repeatedly, do not keep resetting it. This indicates a serious electrical problem.

fixSteps.title: Check for Obstructions
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure condenser fan blade spins freely and is not hitting anything.

fixSteps.title: Professional Diagnosis
fixSteps.instruction: A tech must check compressor amp draw, capacitor, and electrical connections.

whenToCallPro: For any overcurrent condition. Electrical issues require professional diagnosis.

faq.q: Can overcurrent damage the AC?
faq.a: Yes, it can burn out the compressor motor if the protection does not work quickly enough.

faq.q: Should I keep resetting the breaker?
faq.a: No. Repeated trips indicate a real electrical problem. Continuing to reset can cause fire or equipment damage.

faq.q: What is a capacitor?
faq.a: A capacitor stores electrical energy to help the compressor motor start. Failed capacitors are a common cause of compressor overcurrent.

title: Communication Error — Indoor/Outdoor
shortDescription: Communication between indoor and outdoor units has failed.
fullDescription: E6 indicates a communication failure between the indoor and outdoor units. The wiring between units may be damaged or a control board may have failed.
safetyWarning: Turn off both units at their disconnect switches before inspecting wiring.

causes.cause: Loose or damaged communication wire between units
Control board failure in either unit
Power supply issue to one unit

symptoms: AC does not respond to thermostat
One unit runs but other does not

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle Both Units
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off both indoor and outdoor units at their disconnects for 2 minutes, then restore power.

fixSteps.title: Check Communication Wire
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the signal/communication wire running between indoor and outdoor units for damage, loose connections, or corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Verify Power to Both Units
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm both units have power by checking their disconnect switches and breakers.

fixSteps.title: Professional Diagnosis
fixSteps.instruction: If power cycling and wire checks do not resolve it, a control board may need replacement.

whenToCallPro: If power cycling and wire checks do not resolve the communication error.

faq.q: Can a power outage cause E6?
faq.a: Yes, power surges or outages can disrupt communication. Try power cycling both units.

faq.q: Which wire is the communication wire?
faq.a: It is typically a low-voltage wire separate from the power wiring. Check your installation manual for the specific wire.

faq.q: How much is a control board?
faq.a: $150-$500 for the part depending on the unit.

title: Refrigerant Shortage / Low Charge
shortDescription: System has detected insufficient refrigerant charge.
fullDescription: F0 on some Carrier AC systems indicates the system has detected an undercharge condition. Refrigerant is low, typically due to a leak in the system.
safetyWarning: Refrigerant is a regulated substance. Only EPA-certified technicians can legally handle refrigerant.

causes.cause: Refrigerant leak at a connection or coil

symptoms: AC runs but house stays warm

fixSteps.title: Check for Obvious Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Look for oil stains or ice at refrigerant connections and coils, which can indicate leak locations.

fixSteps.title: Check Air Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the filter to ensure good airflow, which can mask refrigerant issues.

fixSteps.title: Turn Off to Prevent Damage
fixSteps.instruction: Running with low refrigerant can damage the compressor. Turn off the AC until repaired.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: A tech must locate the leak, repair it, and recharge the system.

whenToCallPro: Immediately for any refrigerant shortage code.

faq.q: Is topping off refrigerant a fix?
faq.a: No, adding refrigerant without finding and fixing the leak means it will just leak out again.

faq.q: How long does a leak repair take?
faq.a: 1-4 hours depending on the leak location. Some leaks are simple connection tightening, others require coil replacement.

faq.q: Is low refrigerant dangerous?
faq.a: It is not directly dangerous to people but can destroy the compressor, which is the most expensive component.

title: High/Low Voltage Protection
shortDescription: Supply voltage is outside the acceptable range.
fullDescription: P1 indicates the voltage supplied to the outdoor unit is too high or too low for safe operation. The system shuts down to protect electrical components.
safetyWarning: Voltage issues can be caused by utility problems or wiring defects. Do not attempt to modify electrical connections.

causes.cause: Undersized wiring to the outdoor unit
Loose electrical connections causing voltage drop
Faulty voltage sensor on the control board

symptoms: May coincide with utility power events
Lights in home may dim when AC tries to start

fixSteps.title: Wait and Retry
fixSteps.instruction: If caused by a temporary voltage fluctuation, wait 30 minutes and restart the AC.

fixSteps.title: Check Disconnect
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the outdoor disconnect switch for loose connections or corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Monitor Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: If you have a multimeter, check voltage at the outdoor unit disconnect. It should be within 10% of the nameplate rating (typically 208-230V).

fixSteps.title: Call Electrician or HVAC Tech
fixSteps.instruction: If voltage is consistently out of range, an electrician needs to check your electrical panel and wiring.

whenToCallPro: If the error recurs or if you measure voltage outside the acceptable range.

faq.q: Can brownouts cause P1?
faq.a: Yes, utility brownouts or overloaded circuits can drop voltage below the safe range.

faq.q: Should I worry about one occurrence?
faq.a: A single occurrence during a storm or utility event is normal. Recurring P1 errors need investigation.

faq.q: Can I install a voltage stabilizer?
faq.a: Yes, a voltage stabilizer or surge protector can help in areas with frequent voltage fluctuations.

title: Indoor Fan Motor Protection
shortDescription: The indoor blower fan motor has triggered overcurrent or stall protection.
fullDescription: H6 indicates the indoor fan motor is drawing excessive current or has stalled, triggering protection to prevent motor damage or fire.
safetyWarning: Turn off the system before inspecting the indoor fan. A stalled motor can overheat and present a fire risk.
symptoms: Indoor unit makes humming or clicking sound

fixSteps.title: Turn Off
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the AC immediately to prevent motor overheating.

fixSteps.title: Check for Obstructions
fixSteps.instruction: Open the indoor unit panel and check if the fan blade is blocked by debris or ice.

fixSteps.title: Spin Fan Manually
fixSteps.instruction: With power off, try to spin the fan blade by hand. If it resists or grinds, the motor bearings are failing.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: Fan motor replacement requires a technician on most Carrier systems.

whenToCallPro: If the fan does not spin freely or the motor makes abnormal sounds.

faq.q: Can I run the AC without the indoor fan?
faq.a: No, without the indoor fan the evaporator will freeze and the compressor can be damaged.

faq.q: How much is a fan motor?
faq.a: $150-$400 for the part; $200-$600 installed.

faq.q: What causes fan motor failure?
faq.a: Age, dust buildup, and running with restricted airflow accelerate bearing wear.

title: Compressor Locked Rotor
shortDescription: The compressor motor is unable to start — mechanically locked or electrically stalled.
fullDescription: L1 indicates the compressor attempted to start but the motor could not rotate (locked rotor condition). This puts extremely high current draw on the circuit.
safetyWarning: A locked rotor compressor draws dangerous current levels. Turn off the AC at the disconnect immediately to prevent fire or breaker damage.

fixSteps.title: Shut Off Immediately
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the AC at the disconnect switch to prevent further damage.

fixSteps.title: Wait and Retry Once
fixSteps.instruction: Wait 30 minutes for the compressor to cool, then try restarting. A one-time occurrence may be from a brief power fluctuation.

fixSteps.title: Listen on Restart
fixSteps.instruction: If the compressor hums loudly but does not start on retry, it is likely seized or the capacitor has failed.

fixSteps.title: Professional Diagnosis
fixSteps.instruction: A tech must test the capacitor, compressor winding resistance, and attempt a hard start.

whenToCallPro: If the compressor does not start after one retry. Locked rotor requires professional diagnosis.

faq.q: Is a locked compressor repairable?
faq.a: Sometimes a hard-start kit or capacitor replacement can fix it. If the compressor is mechanically seized, it needs replacement.

faq.q: How much is a new compressor?
faq.a: $1,500-$3,500+ installed. Check your warranty coverage.

faq.q: Can I add a hard-start kit?
faq.a: A professional can install a hard-start kit to help aging compressors start. This costs $100-$250.
